content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural delay of the selection age (Finnish/Polish model)'. Early selection at age 10–12 into the 6- and 8-year grammar school tracks is justifiable only if Hungary can sustain politically durable institutional design, ensure between-school equity gains translate into individual-level SES equalization, and maintain implementation continuity across electoral cycles—outcomes Poland achieved partially and lost entirely, demonstrating that the benefits of delayed selection (1 SD gain for at-risk students, improved labor-market outcomes, reduced school segregation) outweigh the benefits of early tracking only if political reversibility risk and individual-SES gains (not achieved in Poland) are weighted equally with test-score gains.
opportunities: Potential achievement gains in the range of Poland's observed 0.16-0.28 SD across subjects, with the largest gains for at-risk/vocational-track-bound students, if Hungary's context proves sufficiently comparable.

Reduced intergenerational income elasticity, by analogy with Finland's observed 23% reduction (0.30 to 0.23) following its delay of tracking from 11 to 16, though Finland's effect was bundled with welfare-state expansion and teacher-education reform not isolated from the tracking-age effect itself.
strengths: Poland's 1999 delayed-tracking reform, which extended comprehensive general education from 8 to 9 years (moving selection from age 12 to age 15), produced PISA score gains of 0.25 SD in mathematics, 0.28 SD in reading, and 0.16 SD in science between 2000 and 2006; students in likely-vocational tracks gained approximately one standard deviation (~100 PISA points).
Between-school equity improved markedly after the 1999 reform: Poland reduced performance disparities between schools from one of the largest in the OECD to one of the smallest.
threats: The scenario identifies a substantial reversal risk citing the Polish precedent (reform reversed after 18 years despite documented gains), and the only entrenchment device contemplated (a cross-party political pact) is not specified with statutory safeguards, independent monitoring triggers, or supermajority requirements, leaving the reform structurally exposed in the same way Poland's was.

Fidesz's immediate framing of any 'unified model' as totalitarian ('no example since communism since') establishes this scenario as the highest-visibility target for culture-war opposition among all five, given it most closely matches the minister's initially floated (then retracted) unified four-year model idea.
weaknesses: The precise causal decomposition between the 1999 reform's structural effect (unified intake) versus its resource/composition effects (investment and teacher change) remains uncertain; even Jakubowski et al.'s two-thirds/one-third split is an estimate and could shift with different methodologies.

Whether individual-level SES gaps could have been closed by longer-duration exposure to the 1999 reform (the Polish cohort was measured at age 15, not adulthood) or whether structural timing reform alone is insufficient without targeted SES-based support programs remains untested.
